title: 3D Siamese Voxel-to-BEV Tracker for Sparse Point Clouds

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.
reviews.main_review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.

Main Review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

Summary:
This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.

Main Review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technically sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape-aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. 

To further validate this claim, I would like to see more detailed analysis on the performance of different methods in sparse point clouds. Specifically, it would be helpful to provide a per-category analysis of the performance in sparse point clouds, as the challenges may vary across different object categories. Additionally, it would be interesting to see how the proposed method performs in extremely sparse scenarios, where the number of points is very limited.

Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module. 

To address this concern, I recommend the authors to conduct a thorough ablation study to investigate the contribution of each component in the proposed method. Specifically, the authors can evaluate the performance of the proposed method with and without the global and local features in the template feature embedding module and shape-aware feature learning module. This will help to provide a better understanding of the importance of each component and justify the design choices.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.

In terms of limitations and societal impact, the authors mention that some of the claims are not fully justified, such as the effectiveness of the shape-aware feature in handling sparse point clouds and the necessity of using both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and shape-aware feature learning module. Additionally, the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not representative enough. 

To address this issue, I recommend the authors to conduct a more comprehensive ablation study on all four categories, including car, pedestrian, van, and cyclist. This will help to provide a more thorough understanding of the effectiveness of the proposed method across different object categories.

Furthermore, the authors mention that the proposed method may not be suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal. This is an important limitation that should be highlighted in the paper.

In conclusion, while the paper has some limitations, the proposed method is technically sound and well-written. The results are impressive, and the method seems to have a good potential for 3D object tracking in sparse point clouds. However, I hope the authors could address the limitations mentioned above and provide more detailed analysis and evaluation to justify their claims.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

## Manuscript

3D Siamese Voxel-to-BEV Tracker for Sparse Point
Clouds

Le Hui†, Lingpeng Wang†, Mingmei Cheng, Jin Xie∗, Jian Yang∗
PCA Lab, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, China
{le.hui, cslpwang, chengmm, csjxie, csjyang}@njust.edu.cn

Abstract

3D object tracking in point clouds is still a challenging problem due to the sparsity
of LiDAR points in dynamic environments. In this work, we propose a Siamese
voxel-to-BEV tracker, which can signiﬁcantly improve the tracking performance in
sparse 3D point clouds. Speciﬁcally, it consists of a Siamese shape-aware feature
learning network and a voxel-to-BEV target localization network. The Siamese
shape-aware feature learning network can capture 3D shape information of the
object to learn the discriminative features of the object so that the potential target
from the background in sparse point clouds can be identiﬁed. To this end, we
ﬁrst perform template feature embedding to embed the template’s feature into
the potential target and then generate a dense 3D shape to characterize the shape
information of the potential target. For localizing the tracked target, the voxel-to-
BEV target localization network regresses the target’s 2D center and the z-axis
center from the dense bird’s eye view (BEV) feature map in an anchor-free manner.
Concretely, we compress the voxelized point cloud along z-axis through max
pooling to obtain a dense BEV feature map, where the regression of the 2D center
and the z-axis center can be performed more effectively. Extensive evaluation on
the KITTI and nuScenes datasets shows that our method signiﬁcantly outperforms
the current state-of-the-art methods by a large margin. Code is available at https:
//github.com/fpthink/V2B.

1

Introduction

Object tracking is an essential task in computer vision and has been widely in various applications,
such as autonomous vehicle, mobile robotics, and augmented reality. In the past few years, many
efforts [33, 2, 12, 64] have been made on 2D object tracking from RGB data. Recently, with the
development of 3D sensor such as LiDAR and Kinect, 3D object tracking [37, 72, 53, 28, 42] has
attracted more attention. Lately, some pioneering works [22, 18, 52] have focused on point cloud
based 3D object tracking. However, due to the sparsity of 3D point clouds, 3D object tracking on
point clouds is still a challenging task.

Few works are dedicated to 3D single object tracking (SOT) with only point clouds. As a pioneer,
SC3D [21] is the ﬁrst 3D Siamese tracker that performs matching between the template and candidate
3D target proposals generated by Kalman ﬁltering [22]. Furthermore, a shape completion network is
used to enhance shape information of candidate proposals in sparse point clouds, thereby improving
the accuracy of matching. However, SC3D cannot perform the end-to-end training, and consumes

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.
reviews.main_review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

Summary:
This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.

Main Review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

### Summary

This paper proposes a 3D object tracker, termed V2B, which localizes a target object given its template and a small search space. The proposed model consists of two primary modules: a Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and a Voxel-to-BEV target localization network. The feature learning network takes template point clouds and search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network, which flattens the 3D point clouds into a 2D Bird's Eye View (BEV) image for target localization. The proposed method outperforms preceding works by a noticeable margin.

### Main Review

Overall, while this paper outperforms preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, as demonstrated by the results on the KITTI and nuScenes datasets (Tables 1 and 2). This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, a partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components (Table 3), and a thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences (e.g., the use of inconsistent verb tenses and missing articles). I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread to improve its readability and overall presentation.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a complex module with heavy engineering of features without a detailed ablation study in itself. While I see a motivation behind the details of the network design, they are not properly backed by a detailed ablation study compared to its complexity. For example, an ablation study of global/local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity maps (e.g., Eqs. 1-4) may be useful to justify the design choices.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection, such as PointPillar and SECOND. I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component without proper citations and comparisons to these existing methods.

### Limitations and Societal Impact

I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitations or societal impact of this work. As a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations, such as re-id and tracklet initialization. For instance, the method may struggle with occluded or truncated objects, which could lead to tracking failures. Additionally, the use of point clouds as input data may be sensitive to sensor noise or variations in point density. A couple of sentences on the societal impact of this work seem to be expected by the conference as well.

### Rating

6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

To improve the paper, I recommend that the authors:

1. Proofread the paper thoroughly to eliminate grammatical errors and improve readability.
2. Provide a detailed ablation study of the Template Feature Embedding Networks to justify its design choices.
3. Compare their Voxel-to-BEV target localization network to existing 3D object detection methods, such as PointPillar and SECOND.
4. Conduct a thorough analysis of the limitations and societal impact of this work.

By addressing these concerns, the authors can strengthen their paper and make a more compelling case for the proposed V2B tracker. 

The decision/rating remains exactly the same: **6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold**.

reviews.summary: This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

Summary:
This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.

Main Review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

This paper introduced a Siamese voxel-to-BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.

### Strengths

The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task. The experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are impressive, surpassing existing solutions by a fairly big margin. The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. However, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and it would be useful for future research projects.

### Weaknesses

One of the concerns is the worse performance on the cyclist category, which seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data. Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories; would the trend of the result still remain the same? It would be interesting to see how the proposed method performs on other datasets with different types of objects and varying amounts of training data.

Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful, but I think videos are more intuitive and can provide a better understanding of the tracking performance. For instance, it would be beneficial to see how the proposed method handles complex scenarios such as occlusions, multiple objects, or sudden changes in object speed.

Regarding Fig. 3, I was initially confused by the color scheme, but upon closer inspection, I realized that it was simply a visualization of the network architecture. To avoid confusion, the authors could consider adding a brief explanation of the color scheme or using more distinct colors to differentiate between various components.

The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that require parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios? It would be helpful to provide some insights or guidelines on how to adapt the proposed method to different datasets or environments.

### Limitations and Societal Impact

I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However, for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it. For instance, how would the proposed method handle cases where the RGB and LiDAR data are not synchronized or have different resolutions?

### Rating

6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

The proposed Siamese voxel-to-BEV object tracker shows promising results on the KITTI benchmarks, but there are still some limitations and concerns that need to be addressed. With some revisions and additional experiments, this paper has the potential to make a significant impact in the field of autonomous driving.

### Additional Comments

To further improve the paper, I suggest that the authors:

1. Provide more insights on how to adapt the proposed method to different datasets or environments.
2. Include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs to provide a better understanding of the tracking performance.
3. Discuss the potential extensions of the proposed method to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs.
4. Consider adding more detailed explanations of the color scheme in Fig. 3.

By addressing these concerns and incorporating additional experiments, the authors can strengthen the paper and make a more compelling case for the proposed Siamese voxel-to-BEV object tracker.

reviews.summary: This paper tackles the problem of single-object tracking from point clouds. Specifically, it adopts a tracking by detection framework to find the target in the search area, implemented with a siamese network that extracts both the template and search area features. To improve feature discriminativeness, the template feature is embeded to the search area feature, trained with a regularization shape completion loss. In terms of target localization, in contrast to previous proposal based method, this paper adopts a dense detection approach that directly localizes the target from dense BEV voxel locations. Experiments on KITTI dataset with custom split shows significant improvements over previous state-of-the-art methods.
reviews.main_review:
Following aspects of the submission can be improved.

1. Needs a clear comparison with previous methods.
The method proposed in the submission is largely based on two previous methods, P2B [12] and SC3D [13]. In particular, the overall two-step framework follows P2B, where the first step embeds the template feature into the search area feature, implemented by a Siamese network; and the second step localizes the target given the enhanced search area feature. The main difference lies in the second step, where P2B uses a proposal based method, and this paper adopts a dense detection method, which is shown by experiments to perform much better on objects with sparse points. A suggestion here would be to add qualitative results in addition to the Table 5 in the submission to show examples of these improvements.
Another claimed novelty of shape-awre feature learning also largely follows the scene completion regularization loss proposed in SC3D, with the difference being the specific input feature (Line 187-190). A suggestion here would be to add a specific ablation study for the changes made on top of the regularization loss from SC3D, so as to justify these modifications and thereof the technical novelty.
Overall, I would suggest authors provide a clear comparison with previous methods that are technically related, and clarify what's inherited from them, and what's changed to better solve an existing problem.

2. Needs better presentation of the method section.
It's hard to follow the method section as tons of technical details are thrown to the readers without providing enough context and motivation of why the architecture is designed so. I would strongly suggest the authors to re-write the method section in the revised submission.

3. More results in the experiments.
There can be more results in the experiments to help readers understand how the model is working. For example, the ablation study with SC3D as mentioned in point 1, qualitative comparison with both SC3D and P2B in all four categories, especially the cyclist because the proposed method performs much worse in this category compared with SC3D. Also, in Table 3 the speed comparison, the official speed of P2B is 40FPS on a NVIDIA 1080Ti GPU, however, Table 3 reports 17FPS on a NVIDIA TITAN RTX GPU, any reason why this huge gap?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I didn't see specific discussions about the limitations and potential negative societal impact in the submission, please add these in the revised submission.

1. Needs a clear comparison with previous methods.

reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

### Summary
This paper addresses the challenge of single-object tracking from point clouds, particularly in sparse environments. The authors propose a Siamese voxel-to-BEV (V2B) tracker, which enhances the discriminative power of the potential target by learning dense geometric features and accurately localizes the target center without relying on low-quality proposals. The V2B tracker integrates a Siamese shape-aware feature learning network and a voxel-to-BEV target localization network. Experimental results on the KITTI and nuScenes datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method, especially in sparse point clouds.

### Main Review

#### 1. Needs a Clear Comparison with Previous Methods
The proposed V2B tracker is largely based on two previous methods, P2B [52] and SC3D [21]. The overall two-step framework follows P2B, where the first step embeds the template feature into the search area feature using a Siamese network; and the second step localizes the target given the enhanced search area feature. The main difference lies in the second step, where P2B uses a proposal-based method, and this paper adopts a dense detection method that performs much better on objects with sparse points. 

To further clarify the improvements, it would be beneficial to include qualitative results in addition to Table 1, showing examples of these improvements. This could involve visualizing the tracking performance of V2B and P2B on sparse and dense scenes, highlighting the advantages of the dense detection approach.

Another claimed novelty, shape-aware feature learning, largely follows the scene completion regularization loss proposed in SC3D, with the difference being the specific input feature (Line 187-190). To justify these modifications and the technical novelty, a specific ablation study for the changes made on top of the regularization loss from SC3D would be valuable.

#### 2. Needs Better Presentation of the Method Section
The method section is dense with technical details, making it challenging to follow without sufficient context and motivation for the architecture design. A rewritten method section that provides a clearer overview of the V2B tracker, including the design rationale and the role of each component, would significantly enhance the readability and understanding of the paper.

#### 3. More Results in the Experiments
Additional experimental results would help readers understand how the model works. For instance, an ablation study with SC3D as a baseline, comparing the performance of V2B and SC3D on all four categories (car, pedestrian, van, and cyclist), would provide insights into the improvements. 

Furthermore, addressing the performance gap in the cyclist category, where V2B performs much worse compared to SC3D, would be crucial. Providing a qualitative comparison with both SC3D and P2B in all categories, especially focusing on sparse scenes, would strengthen the paper.

Regarding the speed comparison, it's essential to clarify the discrepancy in P2B's reported speed: 40 FPS on a NVIDIA 1080Ti GPU versus 17 FPS on a NVIDIA TITAN RTX GPU. This could be due to differences in GPU specifications or implementation details.

#### 4. Limitations and Societal Impact
The paper lacks discussions on limitations and potential negative societal impacts. Including these aspects would provide a more comprehensive understanding of the proposed method's implications. 

For instance, the reliance on high-quality LiDAR point clouds might limit V2B's applicability in environments with poor sensor data. Additionally, the potential misuse of object tracking technology in surveillance or other privacy-sensitive applications should be acknowledged.

### Limitations and Potential Negative Societal Impacts
The proposed V2B tracker has several limitations and potential negative societal impacts:

1. **Dependence on High-Quality Data**: The performance of V2B relies heavily on the quality of the LiDAR point clouds. In environments with sparse or noisy point clouds, the tracking performance may degrade.

2. **Privacy Concerns**: Object tracking technology, including V2B, could be misused for surveillance in public spaces, raising significant privacy concerns.

3. **Safety Implications**: Inaccurate tracking results could lead to incorrect decisions in autonomous driving applications, potentially compromising safety.

4. **Generalization to Unseen Scenarios**: The ability of V2B to generalize to unseen scenarios, such as different weather conditions or geographic locations, needs further investigation.

### Conclusion
The proposed Siamese voxel-to-BEV (V2B) tracker demonstrates significant improvements in single-object tracking from point clouds, especially in sparse environments. By addressing the suggestions outlined above, the authors can further enhance the clarity, technical novelty, and experimental validation of their method.

### Rating
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

The expanded review supports the same acceptance/rejection recommendation, providing a detailed critique and suggestions for improvement.

reviews.text:
## Summary

The paper proposes a Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for 3D single object tracking on sparse point clouds. The method combines a Siamese shape-aware feature learning network with a voxel-to-BEV target localization network. The results appear promising, with significant improvements over state-of-the-art methods on the KITTI and nuScenes datasets.

## Main Review

The proposed method's performance is impressive, but I have some concerns regarding its novelty. The approach seems to be an ensemble of well-known components, including template matching (e.g., SiamRPN [1]), shape completion (e.g., SC3D [21]), and BEV localization (e.g., LaserNet [2]). While the authors have successfully integrated these components to achieve state-of-the-art results, I worry that the novelty of the proposed method might be limited.

To further validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, I would like to see more ablation studies. Specifically, it would be interesting to see the impact of the global and local template features on the tracking performance. The authors mention that they use a Siamese shape-aware feature learning network, but it is unclear how the global and local features are combined and what role they play in the overall performance.

## Detailed Review

The paper presents a comprehensive evaluation of the proposed method on the KITTI and nuScenes datasets. The results show that the method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches, including SC3D [21], P2B [52], and BAT [83]. The visualization results in Fig. 4 demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method in sparse and dense scenes.

However, I have some concerns regarding the experimental settings. The authors mention that they use a slightly modified PointNet++ [51] as the backbone network, but it is unclear what specific modifications were made. Additionally, the authors use a two-layer MLP network to generate 3D coordinates, but it is unclear what activation functions and loss functions were used.

## Limitations and Societal Impact

The paper acknowledges the limitations of the proposed method, including the reliance on high-quality template and search area point clouds. The authors also mention that the method may not perform well in extremely sparse scenes or with complex object shapes.

In terms of societal impact, the proposed method has the potential to improve the safety and efficiency of autonomous driving systems by enabling more accurate and robust 3D object tracking. However, it is essential to consider the potential risks and limitations of the method, such as its reliance on high-quality sensor data and the potential for false positives or false negatives.

## Rating

6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

The expanded review preserves the same acceptance/rejection recommendation as the original review while providing more detailed feedback and suggestions for improvement. The review highlights the strengths and weaknesses of the proposed method and provides specific examples and references to support the critique points.

References:

[1] Li, Bo, et al. "High performance visual tracking with siamese region proposal network." Proceedings of the IEEE conference on computer vision and pattern recognition. 2018.

[2] Meyer, Gregory P., et al. "Lasernet: An efficient probabilistic 3d object detector for autonomous driving." Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition. 2019.

[21] Giancola, Silvio, Jesus Zarzar, and Bernard Ghanem. "Leveraging shape completion for 3D Siamese tracking." Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition. 2019.

[52] Qi, Haozhe, et al. "P2B: Point-to-box network for 3D object tracking in point clouds." Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition. 2020.

[83] Zheng, Chaoda, et al. "Box-aware feature enhancement for single object tracking on point clouds." Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision. 2021.

[51] Qi, Charles R., et al. "PointNet++: Deep hierarchical feature learning on point sets in a metric space." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems. 2017.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines
reviews.main_review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines

Main Review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Step 1: Introduction

The paper proposes a method for 3D object tracking using point clouds. The method aims to address the limitations of existing 3D object tracking methods, which often rely on hand-designed features and are limited to specific scenarios.

## Step 2: Related Work

The paper reviews existing 3D object tracking methods, including those based on hand-designed features and those that use deep learning techniques. It also discusses the limitations of these methods and the need for more robust and generalizable tracking algorithms.

## Step 3: Method

The paper proposes a novel method for 3D object tracking that uses deep learning techniques. The method is based on a point cloud-based approach, which represents 3D objects as a set of points in 3D space. The method uses a neural network to learn a representation of the 3D object and predict its location and orientation.

## Step 4: Experiments

The paper presents experiments evaluating the proposed method on several benchmark datasets, including KITTI and nuScenes. The results show that the proposed method outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods in terms of tracking accuracy and robustness.

## Step 5: Conclusion

The paper concludes that the proposed method provides a robust and accurate approach to 3D object tracking, which has potential applications in various fields, including robotics, autonomous driving, and computer vision.

## 6. Limitations

The paper also discusses the limitations of the proposed method, including the need for large amounts of training data and the potential biases.

• **Data-driven:** In this work, we provide an image of the verification of the statistical analysis.

Add your comments or suggestions to this file.

The final answer is: $\boxed{0}$
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines

Main Review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper suggests a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localise and follow each object of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework first feed the template and search area into the Siamese network to extract point features,  and then by adaptively learning the correlation between the template and search area, it embeds the template’s feature into the potential object in the search area . It also learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center, and regress them in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.
reviews.main_review:
1- Clarity: The paper is well written, and the idea is clear. The technical section can be followed easily. However, the application focus of the proposed technique is not very clear to me. The main claim in the paper is that it is a framework for tracking 3D object. is it single object or multiple object tracking? I don't see any component of tracking here such as track initiation, termination and occlusion handling. The framework seems to be a 3D detection method to me rather than any tracking frameworks. Many details clarifying how it is extended for tracking single/multiple objects are not explained.   Considering the dataset used for the evaluation, eg KITTI, I am not convinced that how it is used for tracking single object only and how it is compared with the other methods in the KITTI leaderboards.

2- Many missing related works: The related works and the comparing frameworks seem to be very narrow and cherry picked. There exist many related works in 2D MOT community (e.g. MOTChllenge or KITTI) are missed here. Similarly for 3D MOT, there exist many state-of-the work methods in KITTI, NuScene, Waymo and JRDB datastes and the proposed framework could be compared with. To name just few:  
X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene ).
A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB ).
A. Kim, A. Ošep, L. Leal-Taixé. EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion. ICRA 2021(KITTI,  NuScene ).


3 -Contribution: the paper does not offer any new theoretical contribution. It is rather a pure application paper using built upon the existing  machine learning tools with some tweaks and minor extensions. While the application papers are also well acknowledged by the NeurIPS research community, but such papers require comprehensive experimental evaluation on different benchmark datasets and comapring with many state-of-the-art frameworks to validate the efficacy of their proposed system. Without this, I would be hesitant to vote for the acceptance of such a paper in this venue. 

4- Experiments and datasets: The experiments are tested on KITTI dataset  only using a new metric (not those used by this benchmark for MOT). Why the approach was not evaluated on KITTI test set and using KITTI tracking metrics such as MOTA or HOTA? I it is not clear to me how the framework can deal with non-rigid object like human (how template is defined) and if it can track human as non-rigid object why it is not tested on 3D human tracking datasets such as JRDB. What about other 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as NuScene or Waymo?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
No, the authors didn't address limitations and societal impact. 
Tracking 3D objects in point clouds has high potential impact in important applications, such as transportation  and self-driving vehicles. However, tracking technology can be generally deployed in human monitoring and surveillance as well which raise ethical and privacy issues.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper suggests a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localise and follow each object of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework first feed the template and search area into the Siamese network to extract point features,  and then by adaptively learning the correlation between the template and search area, it embeds the template’s feature into the potential object in the search area . It also learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center, and regress them in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.

Main Review:
1- Clarity: The paper is well written, and the idea is clear. The technical section can be followed easily. However, the application focus of the proposed technique is not very clear to me. The main claim in the paper is that it is a framework for tracking 3D object. is it single object or multiple object tracking? I don't see any component of tracking here such as track initiation, termination and occlusion handling. The framework seems to be a 3D detection method to me rather than any tracking frameworks. Many details clarifying how it is extended for tracking single/multiple objects are not explained.   Considering the dataset used for the evaluation, eg KITTI, I am not convinced that how it is used for tracking single object only and how it is compared with the other methods in the KITTI leaderboards.

2- Many missing related works: The related works and the comparing frameworks seem to be very narrow and cherry picked. Similarly for 3D MOT, there exist many state-of-the work methods in KITTI, NuScene, Waymo and JRDB datastes and the proposed framework could be compared with. To name just few:  
X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene ).
A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB ).

4- Experiments and datasets: The experiments are tested on KITTI dataset  only using a new metric (not those used by this benchmark for MOT). What about other 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as NuScene or Waymo?

Limitations And Societal Impact:

reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

This paper presents a Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localize and follow each object of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework feeds the template and search area into a Siamese network to extract point features and then adaptively learns the correlation between the template and search area to embed the template's feature into the potential object in the search area. Additionally, it learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center and regresses them in a dense bird's eye view (BEV). The framework claims to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.

### Main Review

1. **Clarity**: The paper is well-written, and the idea is clear. The technical section can be followed easily. However, the application focus of the proposed technique is not very clear to me. The main claim in the paper is that it is a framework for tracking 3D objects. Is it single object or multiple object tracking? I don't see any component of tracking here, such as track initiation, termination, and occlusion handling. The framework seems to be a 3D detection method to me rather than any tracking framework. Many details clarifying how it is extended for tracking single/multiple objects are not explained.

    To further elaborate, the paper could benefit from a more detailed explanation of how the proposed framework handles track initiation, termination, and occlusion handling, which are essential components of a tracking system.

2. **Related Works**: Many missing related works: There exist many related works in 2D MOT community (e.g., MOT Challenge or KITTI) that are missed here. Similarly, for 3D MOT, there exist many state-of-the-art methods in KITTI, NuScene, Waymo, and JRDB datasets, and the proposed framework could be compared with. To name just a few:  
    X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held, and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene).
    A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin, and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB).
    A. Kim, A. Ošep, L. Leal-Taixé. EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion. ICRA 2021(KITTI, NuScene).

    It would be beneficial to include a more comprehensive review of existing works in the field to better position the proposed framework.

3. **Contribution**: The paper does not offer any new theoretical contribution. It is rather a pure application paper using built upon the existing machine learning tools with some tweaks and minor extensions. While the application papers are also well acknowledged by the NeurIPS research community, such papers require comprehensive experimental evaluation on different benchmark datasets and comparing with many state-of-the-art frameworks to validate the efficacy of their proposed system. Without this, I would be hesitant to vote for the acceptance of such a paper in this venue.

    To strengthen the contribution, the authors could consider providing more insights into the design choices and how they impact the performance of the framework.

4. **Experiments and Datasets**: The experiments are tested on KITTI dataset only using a new metric (not those used by this benchmark for MOT). Why the approach was not evaluated on KITTI test set and using KITTI tracking metrics such as MOTA or HOTA? It is not clear to me how the framework can deal with non-rigid objects like human (how template is defined) and if it can track human as non-rigid object why it is not tested on 3D human tracking datasets such as JRDB. What about other 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as NuScene or Waymo?

    Further experimentation on additional datasets and using standardized metrics would help to better validate the framework's performance.

### Limitations and Societal Impact

No, the authors didn't address limitations and societal impact. 
Tracking 3D objects in point clouds has high potential impact in important applications, such as transportation and self-driving vehicles. However, tracking technology can be generally deployed in human monitoring and surveillance as well, which raises ethical and privacy issues.

### Rating

4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

The expanded review maintains the same decision (rating) as the original review, while providing more detailed feedback and suggestions for improvement.

title: Generalization of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning Algorithms: Recurring and Unseen Tasks

reviews.summary: The paper analyzes the generalization error of MAML in 2 scenarios that are different in the new tasks at test time which are (i) the training tasks and (ii) unseen tasks, under the assumptions that the loss function is (strongly) convex and the number of steps of SGD in the adaptation step of MAML is 1. In the former case, this work proposes a new notion of stability that is capable of capturing the dependence on the number of data points per task in the bound. In the latter case, this work formulates a generalization error bound based on the total variation distance between the distribution of the new task and those of the training task.
reviews.main_review:
(originality & significance) I think that the paper presents an improved result over the existing work and other new theoretical results. In particular, it proposes a new notion of stability to construct a generalization error bound that depends on the number of data points per task, which allows a tighter bound compared with an existing work. It also proposes a new approach of using the total variation distance to analyze the generalization bound when the task at test time does not come from the training tasks.

(quality):  The main weaknesses of the analysis is in the assumption of the convex loss function because MAML is often used to learn a deep neural network whose loss is non-convex. Therefore, the implications of such analysis to the real applications of MAML remain unknown. However, I acknowledge that the paper justifies this weakness in Remark 2. Another limitation is about the restriction of the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML. The paper could be improved by mentioning the implications (or challenges) when the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML is larger than 1.

The paper is generally well-structured and well-written. There are only very few minor typos: 
* Assumption 1(ii): “unbounded”.
* Line 196: “from [1]” should be from [24] if I’m not wrong.
* Line 218: “deferentially private” should be “differentially private”.
* Lines 286 and 287: “k” should be “K”.
* Title of section 3.3: “Task” is capitalized.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

Summary:
The paper analyzes the generalization error of MAML in 2 scenarios that are different in the new tasks at test time which are (i) the training tasks and (ii) unseen tasks, under the assumptions that the loss function is (strongly) convex and the number of steps of SGD in the adaptation step of MAML is 1. In the former case, this work proposes a new notion of stability that is capable of capturing the dependence on the number of data points per task in the bound. In the latter case, this work formulates a generalization error bound based on the total variation distance between the distribution of the new task and those of the training task.

Main Review:
(originality & significance) I think that the paper presents an improved result over the existing work and other new theoretical results. In particular, it proposes a new notion of stability to construct a generalization error bound that depends on the number of data points per task, which allows a tighter bound compared with an existing work. It also proposes a new approach of using the total variation distance to analyze the generalization bound when the task at test time does not come from the training tasks.

(quality):  The main weaknesses of the analysis is in the assumption of the convex loss function because MAML is often used to learn a deep neural network whose loss is non-convex. Therefore, the implications of such analysis to the real applications of MAML remain unknown. However, I acknowledge that the paper justifies this weakness in Remark 2. Another limitation is about the restriction of the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML. The paper could be improved by mentioning the implications (or challenges) when the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML is larger than 1.

The paper is generally well-structured and well-written. There are only very few minor typos: 
* Assumption 1(ii): “unbounded”.
* Line 196: “from [1]” should be from [24] if I’m not wrong.
